title: The bench that changed a street
summary: Narrate how a single piece of public furniture — a bench — affected how neighbours interacted on a street or in a neighbourhood.
topic: How a neighbourhood bench changed the way residents connected
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) narrating how the addition (or removal) of a single bench or piece of street furniture changed the social life of a street or neighbourhood. Describe what the street was like before, what changed after, and what this tells you about small design choices. Use past simple to narrate events, present perfect to reflect on lasting effects, and at least one conditional.
hintStarters: Before the bench arrived, the street had always been…
Within a few weeks, something had started to change…
If the city had not installed that bench, I doubt whether…

hintVocab.meaningVi: đồ dùng đường phố (ghế, đèn, thùng rác...)
con phố chỉ để đi qua
nán lại trên một con phố
đi qua mà không dừng lại
đặt một cách có chiến lược
chiếm lĩnh, sử dụng một không gian

sampleText:
For the first three years after I moved to that neighbourhood in Porto Alegre, the street outside my building was pure transit. People arrived, unlocked their doors, and disappeared. Nobody stood on the pavement longer than a minute. There was nowhere to sit, and without somewhere to sit, there was no reason to stay. The street was not unpleasant — there was a tree, and a small corner shop — but it had no grammar for stopping. We recognised each other by our routines — the woman with the small dog at seven in the morning, the man who always had a takeaway container in his hand — but we had never spoken.

In the fourth year, the city installed a single wooden bench under the tree at the corner. I do not know whether it was planned or whether it happened because someone on the district council knew the street. What I know is that within three weeks, the corner had a completely different character. The elderly man from the fourth floor began sitting there on Thursday evenings with a radio. Two teenagers started using it to do homework after school. One afternoon I came home to find four people I had never met having a conversation that had clearly been going on for some time. Nobody had organised this. The bench had done it by giving people a reason to stop and then a reason to stay. You could not have predicted which specific conversations would happen or who would begin them. That was the point — the bench created a possibility, not a programme.

What has changed since then is harder to measure but easy to feel. The street is the same street — the same buildings, the same tree, the same morning and evening light. But the people on it have become a neighbourhood. The woman with the dog knows the man with the takeaway. I have had three proper conversations with the man from the fourth floor and I have learned that he moved here from a town in the south that I happen to have visited. A single piece of outdoor furniture has proved to be a better community tool than anything the district council has officially run. This seems to me an argument that the smallest design decisions — where to put a bench, whether to add a tree, how wide to make a pavement — are not minor. They are the difference between a place where people live and a place where people live together.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Chiếc ghế đã làm được điều đó bằng cách cho mọi người lý do để dừng lại
Những gì đã thay đổi kể từ đó

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to describe a state before the main narrative
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'For the first three years… the street was pure transit' và 'we had never spoken' dùng quá khứ đơn và quá khứ hoàn thành để phân biệt bối cảnh (điều gì đó kéo dài cho đến trước khi có sự thay đổi) với trạng thái hiện tại trong câu chuyện. 'Had never spoken' nhấn mạnh rằng không có tiếp xúc nào xảy ra trước khi cái ghế xuất hiện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'For the first three years… the street was pure transit' and 'we had never spoken' use the past simple and past perfect to separate the background (something that lasted until a change happened) from the current state in the story. 'Had never spoken' stresses that no contact had occurred before the bench arrived.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect to describe a lasting, felt change
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What has changed since then is harder to measure but easy to feel' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nhìn lại từ hiện tại về một sự thay đổi đã xảy ra và vẫn còn hiệu lực. Đây là cách hiệu quả để kết thúc câu chuyện bằng cách phản chiếu lại tác động lâu dài của nó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What has changed since then is harder to measure but easy to feel' uses the present perfect to look back from the present at a change that happened and is still in effect. This is an effective way to end a narrative by reflecting on its lasting impact.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong ba năm đầu sau khi tôi chuyển đến khu phố đó ở Porto Alegre, con phố bên ngoài tòa nhà của tôi chỉ là nơi để đi qua. Mọi người đến, mở khóa cửa rồi biến mất. Không ai đứng trên vỉa hè lâu hơn một phút. Không có chỗ ngồi, và nếu không có chỗ ngồi thì không có lý do gì để ở lại. Chúng tôi nhận ra nhau qua những thói quen — người phụ nữ có con chó nhỏ lúc bảy giờ sáng, người đàn ông luôn cầm hộp đồ ăn mang về — nhưng chúng tôi chưa bao giờ nói chuyện.

Vào năm thứ tư, thành phố lắp đặt một chiếc ghế gỗ dưới cây ở góc phố. Tôi không biết liệu nó có được lên kế hoạch hay không hay nó xảy ra vì ai đó trong hội đồng quận biết con phố. Điều tôi biết là trong vòng ba tuần, góc phố đó có một tính cách hoàn toàn khác. Cụ ông ở tầng bốn bắt đầu ngồi đó vào các tối thứ Năm với chiếc radio. Hai thiếu niên bắt đầu dùng nó để làm bài tập về nhà sau giờ học. Một buổi chiều tôi về nhà thấy bốn người tôi chưa gặp bao giờ đang trò chuyện rõ ràng đã diễn ra được một lúc rồi. Không ai tổ chức điều này. Chiếc ghế đã làm được điều đó bằng cách cho mọi người lý do để dừng lại và sau đó là lý do để ở lại.

Những gì đã thay đổi kể từ đó khó đo lường hơn nhưng dễ cảm nhận. Con phố vẫn là con phố đó — cùng những tòa nhà, cùng cái cây, cùng ánh sáng sáng và chiều tối. Nhưng những người trên đó đã trở thành một cộng đồng láng giềng. Người phụ nữ có con chó biết người đàn ông có đồ ăn mang về. Tôi đã có ba cuộc trò chuyện đàng hoàng với cụ ông ở tầng bốn và tôi đã biết rằng ông chuyển đến đây từ một thị trấn ở miền nam mà tình cờ tôi đã đến thăm. Một chiếc đồ dùng ngoài trời duy nhất đã chứng minh là một công cụ cộng đồng tốt hơn bất cứ điều gì mà hội đồng quận đã chính thức tổ chức. Điều này có vẻ với tôi là một lập luận rằng những quyết định thiết kế nhỏ nhất — đặt ghế ở đâu, có nên thêm một cái cây không, làm vỉa hè rộng bao nhiêu — không phải là nhỏ. Chúng là sự khác biệt giữa một nơi mọi người sống và một nơi mọi người sống cùng nhau.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'without' + gerund to explain the absence of a reason?
options: Without somewhere to sit, there was no reason to stay.
Without somewhere to sit, there was no reason for staying.
Without somewhere to sit, there was no reason of staying.
Without somewhere to sit, there was not any reason to stay.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Without + gerund phrase / noun phrase' is natural. 'No reason to + infinitive' is the standard pattern. 'No reason for staying' and 'no reason of staying' both use unusual prepositions. 'Not any reason to stay' is grammatically correct but less fluent than 'no reason to stay'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'No reason to + infinitive' is the standard pattern — 'for' or 'of' + gerund is non-standard here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect to describe a state that ended when something changed?
options: We had never spoken before the bench arrived.
We have never spoken before the bench arrived.
We never spoke before the bench arrived.
We never had been speaking before the bench arrived.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect 'had never spoken' places the state firmly before the key past event (the bench arriving). Present perfect 'have never spoken' doesn't fit in a past narrative. Simple past 'never spoke' is acceptable but loses the emphasis on the period before the change. 'Never had been speaking' is non-standard.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect emphasises a state that was true right up until a past turning point.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'within' + time phrase to describe how quickly something changed?
options: Within three weeks, the corner had a completely different character.
In three weeks, the corner had a completely different character.
After three weeks, the corner had a completely different character.
During three weeks, the corner had a completely different character.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Within three weeks' emphasises that the change happened at some point before or at the three-week mark — quicker than expected. 'In three weeks' suggests it took exactly three weeks. 'After three weeks' implies it happened once three weeks had passed. 'During three weeks' is unnatural without 'a period of'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Within' suggests the change happened before the time limit expired, implying speed.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'begin' followed by a gerund?
options: The elderly man began sitting there on Thursday evenings.
The elderly man began to sit there on Thursday evenings.
The elderly man begun sitting there on Thursday evenings.
The elderly man begin to sit there on Thursday evenings.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Began + gerund' and 'began + infinitive' are both grammatically acceptable. However, 'begun' is the past participle, not the simple past — 'begun' needs an auxiliary like 'had'. 'Begin' (base form) is wrong in simple past context.
mcQuestions.hint: The simple past of 'begin' is 'began' — 'begun' is the past participle and needs 'had'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect to describe a felt, lasting change?
options: The people on this street have become a neighbourhood.
The people on this street became a neighbourhood.
The people on this street are become a neighbourhood.
The people on this street has become a neighbourhood.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have become' (present perfect) conveys a change that happened and is still the current state. 'Became' (simple past) would suggest it happened at a specific moment and is now over. 'Are become' is incorrect. 'Has become' is singular and doesn't agree with 'the people' (plural).
mcQuestions.hint: 'The people' is plural — use 'have become', not 'has become'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause with 'that' correctly to describe a conversation?
options: A conversation that had clearly been going on for some time.
A conversation which clearly had been going on since some time.
A conversation that clearly were going on for some time.
A conversation that had clearly been going for some time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Had been going on for some time' = past perfect progressive, correct for a process already in progress at the moment of arrival. 'Since some time' is non-standard — use 'for some time'. 'Were going on' has wrong number agreement. 'Had been going' without 'on' drops the phrasal verb particle.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Go on' is a phrasal verb — 'on' cannot be dropped in this context.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional to reflect on what might not have happened?
options: If the city had not installed that bench, we would never have spoken.
If the city didn't install that bench, we would never have spoken.
If the city had not installed that bench, we would never speak.
If the city has not installed that bench, we would never have spoken.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'If + past perfect, would + have + past participle'. Using simple past ('didn't install') in the 'if' clause is second conditional. 'Would never speak' uses the wrong form in the result clause. Present perfect ('has not installed') in the 'if' clause is incorrect for an imagined past.
mcQuestions.hint: Third conditional requires past perfect in the 'if' clause and 'would have + past participle' in the result.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'seem' + adjective complement in the first person?
options: This seems to me an argument for paying attention to small decisions.
This seems to me like an argument for paying attention to small decisions.
This seems for me an argument for paying attention to small decisions.
This seem to me an argument for paying attention to small decisions.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Seem to me + noun phrase' is natural in formal English. 'Seem to me like' is more informal/colloquial. 'Seems for me' uses the wrong preposition — 'to' not 'for'. 'This seem' (without -s) fails subject-verb agreement.
mcQuestions.hint: The pattern 'seems to me + noun phrase' is more formal than 'seems to me like'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'happen to' to express a coincidence?
options: He moved from a town that I happen to have visited.
He moved from a town that I happen to visit.
He moved from a town that I happened to visited.
He moved from a town that I happen visiting.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Happen to + have + past participle' expresses a past action that occurred by coincidence. 'Happen to visit' (present simple infinitive) changes the meaning to a general possibility. 'Happened to visited' uses two past forms incorrectly. 'Happen visiting' is not standard.
mcQuestions.hint: To express a coincidental past action, use 'happen to have + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'A single piece of' as a singular noun phrase?
options: A single piece of outdoor furniture has proved to be a community tool.
A single piece of outdoor furniture have proved to be a community tool.
A single piece of outdoor furnitures has proved to be a community tool.
A single piece of outdoor furniture have been proved to be a community tool.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A single piece of' is singular, so the verb is 'has'. 'Have' is plural and incorrect. 'Furnitures' is wrong — 'furniture' is an uncountable noun with no plural. 'Have been proved' has the wrong auxiliary for a singular subject.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Furniture' is uncountable and has no plural form; 'a single piece of' is always singular.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'the difference between … and …' with noun phrases?
options: They are the difference between a place where people live and a place where people live together.
They are the difference between a place where people live and where people live together.
They are the difference of a place where people live and a place where people live together.
They are the difference between a place where people live from a place where people live together.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The difference between X and Y' — both X and Y must be parallel complete noun phrases. 'Between X and where Y' drops the second noun phrase. 'Difference of' and 'difference between X from Y' use the wrong prepositions.
mcQuestions.hint: 'The difference between' must be followed by two parallel noun phrases joined with 'and'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a street or neighbourhood where people never stop or talk. What is one small, specific change to the physical space — not a programme or an event — that might alter that? What would need to happen for that change to work?
